abstract:
The $k$-Nearest Neighbors ($k$NN) algorithm, first introduced in 1951, serves as a cornerstone of modern data science, with applications spanning recommendation systems, the Internet of Things (IoT), and pattern recognition. As datasets grow in size and complexity, the traditional $k$NN approach faces significant hurdles, including high computational intensity and the "curse of dimensionality." 

This paper provides a systematic review and performance analysis of modifications designed to optimize exact $k$NN techniques, focusing specifically on $k$NN Search and $k$NN Join operations in high-dimensional spaces. We examine 31 $k$NN search methods and 12 $k$NN join methods, categorizing them based on their computing paradigms—I/O-based, main-memory-based, and parallel or distributed architectures. For each methodology, we evaluate its inherent strengths, technical limitations, and practical applicability. To support reproducibility and further experimentation, we provide the source code for the discussed methods. Furthermore, this study identifies persistent challenges in the field and outlines future research directions, such as the integration of ensemble learning and region-based searching to enhance classification accuracy and efficiency for large-scale data tasks. By synthesizing and comparing diverse methodologies, this survey offers a comprehensive guide for researchers and practitioners working with exact $k$NN techniques.
